61, 82 Chapter 1 Introduction 1.1 The three Karpathies in the era of AI

In a 2025 talk Andrej Karpathy said "software is changing -again" and points to three paradigms for software (development): software 1.0 = computer code, software 2.0 = weights (neural networks) and software 3.0 = prompts ("The hottest new programming language is English").foot_0
Lets regard how we would solve a sentiment analysis problem where we want a Python function to return the string "negative", "positive" or "neutral" depending on the sentiment of an input string.
Karpathy 1.0
Solve the problem with 'computer code', here using my afinn library.

With >>> print ( sentiment ( " This is awful " ) ) # negative negative This is really fast, but lacks full understanding of language, e.g., negation is not taken care of >>> print ( sentiment ( ' The film is really not bad ') ) negative
The evaluation ought to have been positive.
Solve the problem by machine learning.
This can be done with different amount of complexity, from simple machine learning models to complex deep learning models, possibly using a already pre-trained model.
Solve the problem with simple machine learning, e.g., logistics regression using scikit-learn.
from nltk .
corpus import movie_reviews from sklearn .
f e a t u r e _ e x t r a c t i o n .
text import Co u nt Ve ct o ri ze r from sklearn .
linear_model import L o g i s t i c R e g r e s s i o n docs = [ ' '. join ( movie_reviews . words ( fileid ) ) for fileid in movie_reviews .
fileids () ] labels = [1 if fileid .
startswith ( ' pos ') else 0 for fileid in movie_reviews .
fileids () ] vectorizer = C o un tV ec t or iz er ( max_features =5000 , binary = True ) X = vectorizer .
fit_transform ( docs ) y = labels model = L o g i s t i c R e g r e s s i o n ( solver = ' liblinear ' , C =0.1) model .
fit (X , y ) def sentiment ( text : str ) -> str : X_test = vectorizer .
transform ([ text ]) pred = model .
predict_proba ( X_test ) [0][1] return ' positive ' if pred > 0.5 else ' negative '

>>> print ( sentiment ( ' this is good ') ) positive >>> print ( sentiment ( ' The film is really lousy ') ) negative Karpathy 2.2 Are more elaborate machine learning approach would use trained deep learning.
As a deep learning model will have many parameters, training requires large amount of annotated data for the system to perform well.
A good sentiment analysis model used 56.6 milliard microposts from Twitter (X) filtered to 1.2 milliard annotated microposts (Felbo et al., 2017).
The annotation was based on emojis.
In other cases, we can start up with a model that has been trained on general language task model and the fine-tune it to the task we want.
In some cases, we may be lucky and find that someone has already done the training for us.
In Hugging Face's transformers Python package there is a "text-classification" pipeline that we can use in the with sentiment analysis.

from transformers import pipeline texts = [ " this is good " , " The film is really lousy " , " The film is not good " ] classifier = pipeline ( " text -cla ssificat ion " ) scores = classifier ( texts ) for text , result in zip ( texts , scores ) : print ( result [ " label " ]. lower () )
while return positive, negative and negative for the three example texts.
In Karpathy 3.0 the problem is solved by prompting.
We rely on an large language model (LLM) runing on some system, local or remote.
import requests def sentiment ( text : str ) -> str : prompt = f """ Classify the sentiment of the following text as positive , negative , or neutral .
Return only the words with no formatting Text : "{ text }" Sentiment : """ response = requests .
post ( ' http :// localhost :11434/ api / generate ' , json ={ " model " : " Gemma3 " , " prompt " : prompt , " stream " : False } ) response .
r a i s e _ f o r _ s t a t u s () result = response .
json () [ " response " ]. strip () .
lower () return ( result if result in ( ' positive ' , ' negative ' , ' neutral ') else ' neutral ' )
In the above example, the sentiment analysis is solved by calling a LLM called Gemma3 running on a local Ollama server, -it could be running anywhere on the Internet provided we have access.
When installed, the Ollama server is runing on port 11434, and if not it can be started with ollama serve
The Python function is merely a wrapper that embeds the text in a prompt, sends it to the LLM and disentagles the result to the required output parameter.

A session may be >>> print ( sentiment ( ' this is good ') ) positive >>> print ( sentiment ( ' The film is really lousy ') ) negative >>> print ( sentiment ( ' The film is really not good ') ) negative
Here there is not that much need of machine learning expertise.
We are "programming" in the English language and use a bit of computer code to glue things together.

If we are using a good LLM we may expect it to perform well for a simple task such as sentiment analysis and indeed in this case the task with a negation, "The film is really not good", it has no problem detecting that the sentiment is negative.
On the negative side the LLM-based approach requires considerable more resources: On my CPU-based laptop with Ollama and Gemma3 one call to the sentiment function takes around one second.

div.head: Natural language processing in the AI era

p.s: The appearance of ChatGPT in November 2022 changed natural language processing (NLP) fundamentally.

Training the large language models requires considerable resources that is often regarded as beyond what is available to individuals and the typical academic environment with researchers and students.
For LLAMA 3 8B and 70B models 7.7M GPU hours was used on NVIDIA H100-80GB hardware.foot_1
This is around 5 GWh, which could (depending on the price of electricity) cost around 5 million Danish kroner.
It is possible to some extent for individuals to create reasonable competitive LLMs.
For Danish Mads Henrichsen of syv.ai paid around 100,000 Danish kroner to train a model for Danish, DanskGPT. 3
Although competitive, it was based on the Llama-3-70B model (Nielsen et al., 2024).
Open vs. closed models.
With proprietary LLMs such as ChatGPT, some of the strongest models have become black boxes.
You do not know what the model actually is, nor whether it will be available tomorrow.
The application programming interface (API) may be deprecated and you can have sudden price changes.
That is in contrast to open-weight models and open-source models.
Open-weight models allow you to download the model and in principle run the model locally, while for open-source models you also know the details of the training.
You have more control over open models and reproducibility might be better.
Traditionally, you would train specialized machine learning systems for each NLP task.
You would build one system for sentiment analysis, another for machine translation, and a third system for text classification.
In contrast, today's general-purpose LLMs can handle very many NLP tasks often requiring only a prompt change.
Abstraction ("just prompting") vs. building from scratch.
Traditionally, you would need substantial machine learning expertise to build a good NLP system.
This created an entry barrier.

With LLMs, many NLP tasks can be solved with a simple API call, so what is now required is software engineering skills (e.g., API integration, prompt engineering) together with machine learning knowledge on how to evaluate the system.
LLM systems use a lot of resources.
Processing many documents may take too long, and purchasing access to strong cloud-based LLMs can be very costly.

Depending on the task, it may be possible to solve a problem with simpler, faster NLP techniques and if this fails, turn to more complex techniques, ending with costly LLMs only if everything else fails.
Lightweight models can handle the easy cases, while large LLM API calls are reserved for the hardest ones, balancing speed, cost, and accuracy.

Python libraries one should be know about NLTK (Natural Language processing Toolkit) described at https://www.nltk.org/ is a classical Python library for natural language processing that is coming from an academic environment.
There is an associated book openly accessible.
It has corpora, lexical resource and fundamental language processing.
It distributes some models, rule-based or machine learning-based, but no fancy neural language models.
An advantage with NLTK is the reasonably light dependency it has.
spaCy at https://spacy.io/ from the Berlin-based ExplosionAI company is a general purpose Open Source natural language processing toolkit branding itself as "industrial-strength".
Although grown out from a more traditional NLP approach it has some deep learning components.
It has the notion of pipelines with a chain of processing can be set up.

Gensim at https://radimrehurek.com/gensim/ is an Open Source Python package from Radim Řehůřek featuring classical information retrieval and topic modeling methods such as bag-of-words (BoW) representation, word2vec and latent semantic indexing (LSI) ( Řehůřek and Sojka, 2010).
A Web service that is using Gemsim is Wembedder at https://wembedder.toolforge.org/
(Nielsen, 2017).
It can handle large corpora by streaming processing.
Scikit-learn described at https://scikit-learn.org is a popular general machine learning library that may also be used in natural language processing contexts.
Part of it have dedicated classes to handle text with perhaps the most important being the CountVectorizer from the sklearn.feature_extraction
module.
This class can take a set of texts and transform them to a matrix.
PyTorch is a library for computation with multidimensional arrays on CPUs or GPUs.
It also features automatic differentiations.
transformers is Hugging Face's library that contains a number of implementations of transformer models and easy way to download model parameters from the Internet.
CampusAI at https://campusai.compute.dtu.dk is a LLM server set up by Technical University of Denmark (DTU) Compute people in 2025.
You will need to be on the DTU network to access the server.
Eduroam and VPN should work.
As a DTU person, you can sign in via Microsoft OIDC.
A human user interface (UI) is available from the Web app.
An LLM Web service is also available.
To get a response from the Web service you need to provide an API key.
This is available from Settings (upper right corner) under Account.
The Swagger API documentation is available at https://chat.campusai.compute.dtu.dk/docs.
In most applications it is a bad idea to write the API key in the code.
This should instead be stated in a separated file.
On a linux system you can but the information an a text file called ~/.env with environmental variables.
For instance,foot_3
1 C A M P U S A I _ A P I _ KE Y = sk -1234567... etc .... 2 CAMP USAI_MOD EL =" Gemma3 " 3 C A M P U S A I _ A P I _ UR L =" https :// chat .
campusai .
compute .
dtu .
dk / api / v1 " In Python, the load_dotenv function in the dotenv Python package will read this information.
You can set a local LLM server up on your own computer.
16 GB or more as well as a GPU would benefit performance.
There are several systems for serving LLMs.
-Ollama is a LLM server that you can setup on your local computer and both a simple text chat interface as well as a LLM API access locally.
To run large models you will need a good amount of memory.
You do not need a GPU-access for the Ollama.
It can run on a CPU, just slower.
A number of commercial LLM API exist.
You pay for how many resources you are using.
The price may change, the models may change or disappear.
A few provide a freemium service with initial free service for students.
-Microsoft Azure can provide some student credits, probably around 100 USD.
-Google can provide student credits.

Nicki Skafte Detlefsen's DTU MLops course uses Google cloud services, so if you plan to take that course you might not want to use the Google credits in this course as you will not have credit left for the MLops course.
Whether the paid LLM APIs are viable for a poor student may depend on the application.

An example of the price for the use of the OpenAI'sfoot_4 GPT-4o model in 2024 for a text-to-query system that used a ReAct-like approach to convert natural language questions to database queries was $0.1253 per question (Liu et al., 2024, section D.1).
One of their datasets, SPINACH, had around 300 questions, so one generation from all questions in the full dataset would cost around 250 Danish kroner.
If prompt optimization is used, e.g., with the optimizers or best-of-N approach in DSPy, then the LLM would be called several times incurring the price of 250 DKK each time.
Speech and Language Processing by Dan Jurafsky and James H. Martin is currently in its 3rd edition as a draft and available online from https://web.stanford.edu/ ~jurafsky/slp3/.
It is a book originally based on "classical" natural language processing, but the recent draft edition contains updates with deep learning models.
It is an equation-based book with very little computer code, only some pseudocode here and there.
I will often point to specific chapters in the Jurafsky-Martin book.
As their 3rd edition is in draft the links might be wrong.

Introduction to Information Retrieval by Christopher D. Manning, Prabhakar Raghavan and Hinrich Schütze (Manning et al., 2008) is available at https://nlp.stanford.edu/IR-book/ information-retrieval-book.html.
I might occasionally point to sections in this book.
Natural Language Processing with Python is a book centered around the NLTK Python library, that is a classical NLP library with no fancy new deep learning.
For simple NLP trickseven in a LLM context-the NLTK may still be relevant.
It is available as a paper book and online at https://www.nltk.org/book/.
I will be using some of the functions and resources in this toolbox.

Natural Language Processing with Transformers, Revised Edition (Tunstall et al., 2022) available from DTU through https://proxy.findit.cvt.dk/login?url=https://ebookcentral. proquest.com/lib/DTUDK/detail.action?docID=7000597 is a book centered around Hugging Face's transformer Python library.
Understanding Deep Learning by Simon J.D. Prince is available from https://udlbook. github.io/udlbook/.
It is used in the Deep Learning course at DTU and has a chapter on transformers (chapter 12).

1.6 Courses DTU prerequisites 01001 Mathematics 02403 Probability 02405 Statistics 02002 Python programming 02450/1/2 Introduction to Machine Learning and Data mining Highly recommended 02465 Machine Learning Operations.
This is Nicki Skafte Detlefsen's course running in January.
02456 Deep learning Recommended 02613 Python for HPC 02462 Signals and Data.
This is Hiba Nassar's course where she introduces, e.g., transformers.
It is only for KIDS students Courses on other universities: Stanford CS336: Language Modeling from Scratch by Percy Liang.
It has a YouTube playlist at https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLoROMvodv4rOY23Y0BoGoBGgQ1zmU_MT_.
There are lengthy assignments in this course, each of a difficulty that corresponds to an entire course.
Chapter 2 Text 2.1 Text encoding Text is encoded with numerical values.
The most common representations are ASCII and UTF-8.
In its basic form, ASCII encodes numbers, upper and lowercase English letters and various other characters as values between 0 and 127.

On the Linux command-line the hexdump program is usually available to display the content of a file in a numerical (e.g., hexadecimal) form This may sometimes be necessary to use if the encoding is not readily know.

ord and chr and names for functions are are often found in programming langugage to convert between the a string type and a numerical type, e.g., in Python converting the character "d" to its ASCII (which is 100) and back

The distribution of the bytes are considerably skewed in a normal text, see Figure 2.1, with the space byte being the most frequent, some lowercase letters with also quite common, but then some type of bytes with no occurence.
For handling non-English characters the upper part of the byte (from 128-255) is used.
Standard Generalized Markup Language (SGML) is a language that has inspired HyperText Markup Language (HTML) and Extensible Markup Language (XML) and it is an ISO standard: ISO 8879:1986.
The ideas of angle-backet tags, entities, attributes and hierarchical structure also appear in HTML and XML.
SGML has been used for some Linux documentation but now seems not to appear much on the open Internet.
First part of the an example 1 reads <! doctype linuxdoc system> <article> <title>First Example ( example1 ) <author>David S .

Lawyer Introduction

This is a very simple example of " source " for the LinuxDoc text formatting system .
This paragraph begins with a paragraph tag ( a " p " enclosed in angle brackets ) .

Here there are the angle-backet tags
, , <author>, <sect> and <p>.
SGML entities are prefixed with an ambersand and suffixed by a semicolon.
For instance, &, <, >, " and ' are encoding &, <, >, " and ', respectively.
They can also be represented with the decimal ASCII number, e.g., & may instead be written as & These entities are carried over to HTML and XML.
In some cases where the encoding/decoding has gone wrong one might see, e.g., & appear in the text when it correctly should have been represented as "&".
HTML is the language for Web pages.
If specific information is to be extracted from an HTML page then instead of using a LLM and other machine learning-based methods a simple approach using the XPath language 2 can be used.
XPath processing is implemented in HTML/XML libraries, such as the lxml Python library.

Say we want to extract the title of the DTU page "Master of Science (MSc) in Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence" 3 Within this document we have the fragment we want to extract <h1 class =" a -heading a -heading --h1 u -margin -bottom --sm "> Master of Science ( MSc ) in Human -Centered Artificial Intelligence </ h1> import requests from lxml import html url = " https :// www .
dtu .
dk / english / education / graduate / msc -programmes / human -centeredartificial -intelligence " response = requests .
get ( url , headers ={" User -Agent ": " faan@dtu .
dk "}) tree = html .
fromstring ( response .
content ) title = tree .
xpath ( '// h1 / text () ') [0].
strip () print ( title ) which print the level one heading "Master of Science (MSc) in Human-Centered Artificial Intelligence" with the XPath specified as //h1/text().

Here // means anywhere in the document, h1 is for the

...

tag(s) in the file and /text() means extraction of the text nodes in the matched part.
Some more examples of XPath extraction are shown in Table 2.3 where the output is based on the HTML in Listing 2.1.
Note there are different versions of XPath with the most recent being 3.1 from 2017.
The lxml library supports XPath 1.0. 4
LLMs can be used to generate the XPath specification from a seed set of Web pages, so the generated XPath can be used over unseen Web pages without the LLM.
In cases where the Web page is not well-form, e.g., with syntax errors the BeautifulSoup Python library will usually help.
This library can use different parser, one of the being lxml.
//h1/@id main-title Attribute selection: get the id attribute of all <h1> elements.

//p[@class="subtitle"]/text() MSc programme Select

elements with a specific class value.
//nav//a/text() Overview; Courses; Admissions // means "any descendant": all links inside <nav>, then their text.
//a[@class="aus"]/@href /admissions Pick the link with class aus and read its href.
//section[@class="facts"]//dd/text() 2 years; English Select all definition values (<dd>) inside the "facts" section.
//section[h2="Courses"]//li/@data-ects 5; 10
Pick the section whose <h2> text is exactly "Courses", then read an attribute.
normalize-space(//section[@class="content"]/p[1]) Learn to build AI systems that work well for people.
First paragraph in the content section, collapsed whitespace.
//a[starts-with(@href,"mailto:")]/text() faan@dtu.dk
Find links whose href begins with mailto:.
//img/@alt Robot and human collaboration
Extract the alt text of all images.
Table 2.1: Example XPath queries and their outputs for the sample HTML.
If you download webpages en masse from the Internet you should consider the comme il faut of scraping, -not just for HTML pages but for all pages.
The robots.txt at the root of the Web server, e.g., https://www.dtu.dk/robots.txt,
specifies in its own language how bot operating on the site should behave.
For instance, Disallow : / sites / specifies that no webpage under the "/sites/" directory should be crawled.
The file may also describe how often the bot can query the Web server
Crawl -delay : 1000
In Python, the urllib.robotparser
module has methods to parse the robots.txt
files and test whether a specific URL is ok to crawl to.
If you are scraping a Website, there might be a file that gives pointers to relevant files to download.
This is the sitemap.xml
at the root of the web server, see https://kurser.dtu.dk/sitemap.xml
for an example.
This file may also be referenced in the robots.txt
file.
When downloading files programmatically you should set the User-Agent header field.
When using the requests Python library this can be done by setting the "User-Agent" key in the headers input argument.
requests .
get ( url , headers ={" User -Agent ": " faan@dtu .
dk "})
In this case, the User-Agent is set to my email, which you obviously should change in your application.
Indeed some Website return an error (Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP) 403 or similar) if this is not set.
For instance, the DTU Web server returns 403 when no User-Agent is set: import requests response = requests .
get (" https :// dtu .
dk ") print ( response .
status_code )
The Web server may send an error message about rate limiting, the 429 Too Many Requests. 5
The client should then stop the downloading from that host temporarily and reduce the download rate.
The server may include a Retry-After in the header that in seconds indicates how long the downloader should wait before making a new request.
Modern Web pages are often dynamically generated: the page includes JavaScript code that fetches data from an endpoint and builds the content.
To view the complete page, it is necessary to execute the JavaScript.
Without executing it, the page may appear incomplete or even empty.
There exist several Python frameworks for handling dynamic Web content, e.g., Selenium and Playwright.
If a Web page is downloaded, then we might want to store when it was downloaded and from which address.
WARC is a format for Web crawls used by Internet Archive and Common Crawl.
An tiny example WARC file can be downloaded from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/webrecorder/pywb/ main/sample_archive/warcs/example.warc.gz.
The warcio Python package will read and write in the format and with the example WARC file one can print, e.g., the address of the downloaded web pages stored in the file

div.head: Extracting the content of a Webpage

p.s: Although there are tools that enable the extracting of all text from a Web page, there might be cases where you are not interested in extracting all the content.
Usually Web pages are filled up with more than the actual content: Menu navigation, links to other pages for you to read, comments, ads, etc.: Boilerplate.
Consider the BBC article "First celestial image unveiled from revolutionary telescope" at https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/cj3rmjjgx6xo.
Besides the content with title it has a header ("BBC", "Register", "Sign in"), a line of navigation ("Home", "News", . . .
), article footer ("Watch BBC News go behind the scenes", . . .
), "Related", "More from the BBC", footer (e.v., "BBC in other language", "Terms of use", . . .
Extracting the actual content may be referred to as cleaning or boilerplate removal and a range of methods are possible.
More generally, we might not just want to identify the "content" (body text) but also some metadata, where author and date of publication could be examples.
There are various Python modules for these operations, e.g., boilerpy3, newspaper3k, Trafilatura, readability-lxml, -somewhat in order of increasing sophistication.
They may have several dependencies on other modules.
There are published benchmark of the performance of HTML extraction libraries, -not just Python (Barbaresi and Lejeune, 2020;Hatwar et al., 2024).

For an author extraction task a study found "no obvious single library that performs at a high level across all languages", but pointed to Trafilatura that often performed the best (least worst) (Hatwar et al., 2024).
For each Web site there might be an appropriate HTML tag that you can use to extract the content.
For the BBC article there is an <article> HTML tag that would be useful.
But this tag may not be available on other Web sites: nrk.no has it, dr.dk does not.
In Wikipedia, the actual content may better be encapsulated with the <main id="content class="mw-body"> tag.
Using the newspaper3k, the above BBC article is handled with
from newspaper import Article url = ' https :// www .
bbc .
com / news / articles / cj3rmjjgx6xo ' article = Article ( url ) article .
download () article .
parse ()
In this case, the metadata is not succesfully extracted: The title in article.title is wrong and neither the authors nor date of publication are extracted at all.
The text in the article.text
variable has a couple of problems: The title is in the first part of the text, boilerplate (publication date, "share", "save") is contaminating and image captions are intermixed with the body text.
The length of a common HTML Web page, with all its formatting, Javascript and so on, may present a challenge for the prompt length restriction of some LLMs.
For instance, the above BBC article has a size of more than 250 KB, so using a standard pre-trained LLM for content extraction may be non-trivial.
Furthermore, the processing of long texts requires a lot of token use that might be expensive in terms of computation, money and/or energy.
One idea that has been explored is to use an LLM to generate extraction code, e.g., generate Python code with XPaths.
This approach can potentially speed up the creation of scraper code, but it still require you find find a way to squeeze the large Web pages into the confines of the LLM window.
There exists an old dataset for evaluation of cleaning web pages, the CleanEval 2007 (Baroni et al., 2008), see https://sigwac.org.uk/cleaneval/.
Another dataset is DAnIEL, see (Barbaresi and Lejeune, 2020).foot_6
XML can be handle with the xml libraries of Python more or less the same way as HTML.
Specific elements can be extracted with the XPath functionality.
The Text Encoding Initiative (TEI) consortium has defined a particular XML format for annotated text documents.
It defines metadata fields such as <title>, <abstract>, <author>, <editor>.
Lingustics segments may on the level of, e.g., sentence, phrase or word with tags such as <s>, <phr> and <w>.
A very simple example of TEI XML file is <TEI xmlns =" http :// www .
tei -c .

org / ns /1.0">

This is the first sentence .</
s> <s>This is the second sentence .</
s> </ p> </ body> </ text> </ TEI> An XPath-based extraction of the sentences with lxml could be from lxml import etree tree = etree .
parse (" example .
tei .
xml ") ns = { ' tei ': ' http :// www .
tei -c .
org / ns /1.0 '} # Name space s e n t e n c e _ e l e m e n t s = tree .
xpath ( '// tei :s ' , namespaces = ns ) sentences = [ etree .
tostring ( element , method = ' text ' , encoding = ' unicode ') .
strip () for element in s e n t e n c e _ e l e m e n t s ] print ("\ n ". join ( sentences ) )
Here there is a namespace used http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0.
If the ns variable was not used we need to write s e n t e n c e _ e l e m e n t s = tree .
xpath ( '//{ http :// www .
tei -c .
org / ns /1.0} s ') TEI XML is used, e.g., by GROBID Portable Document Format (PDF) converter, see section 2.10.1.
LaTex can usually be converted to PDF and the PDF can be converted to, e.g., text or Markdown, see section 2.10.
LaTeX is a dangerous format that can contain fragments that maliciously attack the computer where the latex command is running (Checkoway et al., 2010a).
For instance, \loop\iftrue\repeat in a LaTeX file makes the latex program run infinitely and can be used for denial of service (Checkoway et al., 2010b).
Untrusted LaTeX files must be handled in a special way.
Lightweight markup languages are aimed at making it easy for machines and humans to read and write relatively simple texts.

Ward Cunningham created the first wiki and he used his own lightweight markup language: To create a webpage you did not need to balance tags in HTML but instead write in a simplified text format following simple conventions (Leuf and Cunningham, 2001, Table 4.1, page 83).

Wikipedia was based by the Cunningham approach, but the software that is running Wikipedia, MediaWiki , has diverged from the original Cunningham language to a more complicated and ideosyncratic language with tables, Lua modules, templates, categories which is probably not used in other systems beyond MediaWiki-based wikis.
The idea of lightweight markup language have now been carried on with Markdown and reStructured-Text.
These languages are now used fairly widely, e.g., Numpy's documentation docstrings, GitHub's readme files and ChatGPT's interaction.
MediaWiki text, such as Wikipedia articles presents a special problem.
It is text data in UTF-8, but there are markup in its own MediaWiki-specialized format.
Template may transclude text from other pages.
A particularly challenging MediaWiki are the Wiktionaries that are wiki-based dictionaries.
If you examing raw wikitext of the English Wiktionary, you will see that most of the article pages are built from template, see, e.g., https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/r%C3%B8d.
Various library have been created to handle the MediaWiki format, e.g., WikiTextGraph and mwparserfromhell.
JavaScript Object Notation (JSON) is a language for data.
It was originally meant for communication between a web browser and a server and has grown to become a dominant format for Web services.
It is now also used more widely as a data storage format and not necessarily on the Web.
The structure of JSON maps well with Python's data structures.
Reading JSON in Python can be done with the json library that is available in the Python Standard Library, -there is no need to install extra libraries.
The popular request and httpx libraries for Web page download can parse JSON files.
There is a json method in the response object.
JSON Lines is a special file/streaming format, where each line contains a valid single possibly nested JavaScript object, see https://jsonlines.org/.
The conventional extension is .jsonl
with MIME type application/jsonl.
The advantage with the JSON Lines format that one does not need to read the entire file to process the data, -one line at time (or a batch of multiple lines at a time).
Compressed JSON Lines can also be decompressed and processed on-the-fly.
And even download from the Web can be streamed.
Below is an example with a remote gzipped JSON Lines file with stream processing (in this case you would probably want to download the file to a local directory).
import gzip , json , requests url = " https :// huggingface .
co / datasets / flax -sentence -embeddings / s t a c k e x c h a n g e _ t i t l e _ b o d y _ j s o n l / resolve / main / s m a l l _ s t a c k e x c h a n g e s .
jsonl .
gz " response = requests .
get ( url , stream = True ) response .
r a i s e _ f o r _ s t a t u s () with gzip .
GzipFile ( fileobj = response .
raw ) as f : for i , line in enumerate ( f ) : obj = json .
loads ( line ) title , body = obj .
get ( " texts " , [ " " , " " ]) tags = obj .

get ( " tags " , []) print ( f " \ nEntry { i +1}:\ nTitle : { title }\ nBody : { body [:100]}...\ nTags : { tags } " ) if i + 1 >= 10: break 2.9 Tabular data Tabular data may come as comma-separated values files or spreadsheet files.
In many cases, the Pandas Python library may be able to read tabular data well, and the central functions are read_table and read_excel.
These function can use various engines that can be explicitly specified via the engine option.
The supported engines for read_excel are currently xlrd, openpyxl, odf and pyxlsb.

When processing tabular data and transforming it to a textual representation, you might want to add contextual information, e.g., metadata, table caption, preceeding or following paragraph (if the table is embedded in a text document).
If we want to handle the tabular data in a NLP context there are various ways to process the tabular data.

If the size of tabular data fairly limited we can convert the entire tabular data to a form that can be submitted to a LLM as part of the prompt, e.g., the tabular format of Markdown or as comma-separated values (CSV) (Grijalba et al., 2025).
2. Let an LLM write code that "queries" data, e.g., Python code for Pandas or Numpy (Grijalba et al., 2025).
If the data can be represented in a database this approach this could be referred to as text-to-SQL or text-to-SPARQL when the generated code is in a query language.
3. Convert the tabular data to small pseudodocuments, one for each cell, chunk the data and use the chunks in a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system.
A competitions on question answering over tabular data found that code generation tends to do better than adding the tabular data to the prompt (Grijalba et al., 2025).
species island bill length mm bill depth mm flipper length mm body mass g sex 0 Adelie Torgersen 39.1 18.7 181.0 3750.0
Male 1 Adelie Torgersen 39.5 17.4 186.0 3800.0
Female 2 Adelie Torgersen 40.3 18.0 195.0 3250.0
Female 3 Adelie Torgersen NaN NaN NaN NaN NaN 4 Adelie Torgersen 36.7 19.3 193.0 3450.0
Female Table 2.2: Header of Penguin dataset in Seaborn.
2.9.1 Execution-based handling of tabular data LLM-based systems for handling tabular data may maintain the data in tabular form and construct queries for the data.

A query in a quesion-answering system could be converted to Pandas Python code using LLMs, and generated Python code can be executed in a controlled environment (Grijalba et al., 2025;Site et al., 2025).

Example with the first tabular dataset (a billionaire dataset) in the DataBench datasetfoot_7 with a question "What is the name of the richest person under 40 years?" from datasets import load_dataset import pandas as pd all_qa = load_dataset ( " cardiffnlp / databench " , name = " qa " , split = " train " ) ds_id = all_qa [ ' dataset ' ][0] df = pd .
read_parquet ( f " hf :// datasets / cardiffnlp / databench / data /{ ds_id }/ all .
parquet " ) question = " What is the name of the richest person under 40 years ?
" prompt = f """ Generate Pandas -based Python code for the question : { question } over a Pandas DataFrame ( ' df ') with the columns : { df .
columns } """ print ( prompt )
A copy-paste to ChatGPT 5 may generate the following code.
Executing Python code outside a sandbox can be dangerous.
Malicious questions could change the system if there is no effective guardrails.

div.head: Converting tabular data to small documents

p.s: Tabular data can be converted to a textual format where the column information is available "locally" to the cell values.
An example with the penguin dataset from the Seaborn python library, see
Table 2.2.
import seaborn as sns def df_to_yamlish ( df , obj_name = " Row " ) : docs = [] for i , row in df .
iterrows () : lines = [ obj_name ] for col , val in row .
items () : lines .
append ( f " -{ col }: { val } " ) docs .
append ( " \ n " .
join ( lines ) ) return " \ n \ n " .
join ( docs ) df = sns .

load_dataset ( " penguins " ) print ( df_to_yamlish ( df , " Penguin " ) ) This Python script results in Penguin -species: Adelie -island: Torgersen -bill_length_mm: 39.1 -bill_depth_mm: 18.7 -flipper_length_mm: 181.0 -body_mass_g: 3750.0 -sex: Male Penguin -species: Adelie -island: Torgersen -bill_length_mm: 39.5 -bill_depth_mm: 17.4 -flipper_length_mm: 186.0 -body_mass_g: 3800.0 -sex: Female ... where each individual table row now can be chunked (on double newline "\n\n") and the header information is retained in each chunk.
PDF files are binary files.

In some cases the text may be to a more or less verbatim available in the file, but often the formating in the PDF makes it difficult: Handling of PDF and extracting a text that can be further processed poses special problems Hyphenation.
Line breaking and page breaking breaks words and a dehyphenation step may be needed.
Multiple columns.
PDF files often comes with a two-column output.
The PDF extractor should keep the order of the text and not intermix columns.
Page headers and footers.
The body text in the running document may have interleaved headers and footers with, e.g., page numbers and title.
Interleaved figures and tables.
There may be a difference between what is human visible and what a machine sees.
This has been used to hide prompt injections in scientific articles (Gibney, 2025).
PDF files do not necessarily contain the text as text but rather as images scanned from original document such that optical character recognition is necessarily.
On the Linux et al. platform, several tools are available for conversion of PDF files, e.g., pdftotext.
The less command line program may be able to read and show PDF files as text.
In itself, less does not do the conversion.
Instead the processing is handled via the lesspipe script that calls the pdftotext with the -layout parameter before piping the text result to the less program.
PyMuPDF with documentation at https://pymupdf.readthedocs.io is a Python library for extraction and manipulation of PDF files.
It has been used in the RAG systems, see, e.g., (Cao et al., 2025).
PyMuPDF is included in the community library of LangChain.
There is a version for Markdown extraction called PyMuPDF4LLM and it is installable with pip install pymupdf4llm.
With an example PDF file downloaded with wget https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.15133, a Markdown representation can be obtained from within Python >>> import pymupdf4llm >>> print ( pymupdf4llm .
to_markdown ("230 3.15133" ) ) In this case, it can identify for instance, headers, links and references.
It manages the two-column format of the PDF well.
See further methods at https://pymupdf.readthedocs.io/en/latest/pymupdf4llm/.
pdfplumber is a Python library that can extract tables.
With the PDF file from above import pdfplumber import pandas as pd pdf = pdfplumber .
15133") page = pdf .
pages [2] tables = page .
find_tables () df = pd .
DataFrame ( tables [0].
extract () )
In this case, the columns of the tables in the PDF are extracted correctly, but the rows with no line separation are incorrectly extracted.
Possibly the table_settings for the extraction method can be adjusted.
Otherwise postprocessing of the rows is necessary.
GROBID (GeneRation Of BIbliographic Data) described at https://grobid.readthedocs.io is a PDF extraction library that has a particular focus on technical and scientific documents.
GROBID is a Java program.
GROBID converts PDF to the TEI XML format and it can extract metadata like title, author, reference and body text with segmentation into sentences.
Docker containers are provided which can be pulled from the Docker Hub.
The full version is a multigigabit image with TensorFlow libraries.foot_8
docker pull grobid / grobid :0.8.2 Starting the container docker run --rm --gpus all --init --ulimit core =0 -p 8070:8070 grobid / grobid :0.8.2
If successful the Web application is available at http://localhost:8070/.
There is an associated Web service which can be called.
A Python function could call the Web service with the following code.

def c o n v e r t _ p d f _ t o _ t e i ( pdf_path ) : url = ' http :// localhost :8070/ api / p r o c e s s F u l l t e x t D o c u m e n t ' with open ( pdf_path , ' rb ') as pdf_file : files = { ' input ': ( pdf_path , pdf_file , ' application / pdf ') } response = requests .
post ( url , files = files ) if response .
status_code == 200: return response .
text # TEI XML content else : raise Exception ( f " Error { response .
status_code }: { response .
text } " )
PDF information extraction benchmarks has found that GROBID performs well for extracting the various content from scientific articles against, e.g., PyMuPDF (Meuschke et al., 2023).
An evaluation of GROBID for table extraction found that it performed poorly compared to a transformerbased method, the "Table Transformer" (Ozyurt and Bandrowski, 2025).
With the advent of LLMs, there has been a development of tools that can handle a range of document formats as input and output a simplified format for input to an LLM.
IBM's Docling and Microsoft's MarkItDown are two such type of Python libraries.
These two tools also work as command-line tools once pip-installed.
An example with a PDF file from arXiv download and converted with markitdown to a markdown file
$ wget https :// arxiv .
pdf # renaming the file $ markitdown 2303.15133.
md
The converted markdown file is not without problems.
Some of the lines are mixed up, e.g., this fragment displays mishandled text from the PDF file
Other endpoints than the configured default can an endbe queried.
the Template:SPARQL Mediapoint parameter template on Wikidata to specify the other Wiki endpoint.
Among the dependencies of markitdown is pdfminer.six
and to fix such a problem one would need to have better control the conversion from PDF. PyMuPDF and GROBID could be better alternatives for the PDF conversion.
For the BBC article mentioned above, MarkItDown will convert the HTML to markdown with all the boilerplate of the HTML.

Docling is a general Open Source toolkit for conversion of a range of document formats: PDF, images, Microsoft Office formats and HTML and with export to Markdown, JSON and HTML (Livathinos et al., 2025).
For PDF, Docling uses it own special converter, docling-parse.
For instance, with a scientific article from arXiv in PDF docling 2303.15133. pdf In this case, it automatically determines the file type correctly as a PDF file.
The conversion process ends with Markdown-formatted file call 2303.15133.md.
Compared to MarkItDown the conversion is more successful.
There is a nasty linebreaks within URLs that is not entirely well-handled, e.g., one URL becomes http://scholia.
toolforge.org .
with a few extra spaces.
But otherwise the paragraphs and the two-column layout is well-handled.
There is a considerable number of dependencies for the complete docling library.
My Python virtual environment with docling and its dependencies are over 7 GB.
The dependencies are transformers and lxml Python libraries among many others.

div.head: NLTK text corpora

p.s: For quick access to some example manageable corpora one can use NLTK.
They will need to be downloaded first with import nltk nltk .
download ()
The texts are stored in an nltk_data/corpora subdirectory.
The corpora in NLTK is accessible with the nltk.corpus
submodule. 9
They may be rather small compared to the large corpora used to train LLM.
The text in the corpora may be available with various tags/annotations.
from nltk .
corpus import brown as corpus " ". join ( corpus .
sents () [0]) 2.12.2 Datasets text corpora Hugging Face's datasets Python library is for loading and processing machine learning datasets, especially text datasets.
It can access and download individual datasets from the large set on the Hugging Face Hub.
For instance the popular IMDB movie reviews can downloaded and the first text of the first example in the training set can be shown with

div.formula: from datasets import load_dataset dataset = load_dataset (" imdb ") print ( dataset [" train "][0][ ' text '][:100])

p.s: Here is the output I rented I AM CURIOUS-YELLOW from my video store because of all the controversy that surrounded it w 2.12.3 Tinystories TinyStories is a generated dataset with simple English stories.
In the training set there are 2 million texts, while the validation set has around a managable 22 thousand.
from datasets import load_dataset dataset = load_dataset (" roneneldan / TinyStories " , split = ' validation ') print

div.formula: ( dataset [0][ ' text '])

p.s: The first validation set story is Spot.
Spot saw the shiny car and said, "Wow, Kitty, your car is so bright and clean!" Kitty smiled and replied, "Thank you, Spot.
I polish it every day."
After playing with the car, Kitty and Spot felt thirsty.
They found a small pond with clear water.
They drank the water and felt very happy.
They played together all day and became best friends.
Institutional Books 1.0 (Cargnelutti et al., 2025).
242B tokens from around a 1 million books with almost 400 million pages, see https://www.institutionaldatainitiative.org/institutional-books.
As a Hugging Face dataset it is available as instdin/institutional-books-1.0
Danish Gigaword (Derczynski et al., 2021).
Around 1 million words from Danish texts corresponding to around 10 gigabyte uncompressed data.
Common Crawl: "Over 300 billion pages spanning 18 years".
There exist cleaned Common Crawl datasets.

The Pile. 825 GiB English text corpus consisting of over 20 different datasets, parts of Common Crawl, PubMed Central scientific papers, Stackexchange, Github, Wikipedia are some of the largest sources for the dataset.
When you have a corpus of texts you might want to exclude some of the text: Deduplication and content filtering.
Some applications restrict their corpus to a specific language, so language filtering is necessary (Baack, 2024).

div.head: Chunking

p.s: In some applications you would like to split long documents.
This is the case for passage information retrieval systems and RAG systems.
Often documents of relevance for RAG comes as lengthy texts.

The interesting information may written in a small part of the document and an overall representation of the document would not represent all the many aspect of the information of the document well, so it would be a good idea to break down the document into small meaningful segments.
The initial description of RAG used the Wikipedia dump a split articles into 100-word chunks which resulted in 21 million small documents (i.e., chunks of Wikipedia articles) (Lewis et al., 2020).

Variables in chunking are whether to use fixed size or variable size chunks, whether the chunks should overlap and whether the chunking should be aware of the content, e.g., words, sentences, paragraphs or formatting.
If your text contains markup in, e.g., Markdown or HTML, you might want to avoid splitting in a middle of a tag.
The size of the chunk should be limited by what the language model can handle and it would not be the number of characters in the document, but rather the number of tokens.

Within the langchain Python package, the text_splitter submodules implements a number of text splitters: Character, HTML, JSON, LaTeX, NLTK-based, spaCy-based, token-based splitter and a number of others.
The RecursiveCharacterTextSplitterfoot_10 class splits a string based on a list of separators.
The default for the top split is the double newline, then a newline and a space.
The default chunk size is 4,000 and the default overlap 200.
Here is an example with smaller numbers from langchain .
text_splitter import R e c u r s i v e C h a r a c t e r T e x t S p l i t t e r from datasets import load_dataset dataset = load_dataset ( " t i n y _ s h a ke s p e a r e "

div.formula: ) text = dataset [ ' train ' ][0][ ' text '] splitter = R e c u r s i v e C h a r a c t e r T e x t S p l i t t e r ( chunk_size =200 , chunk_overlap =100) chunks = splitter . split_text ( text ) print ( " \ n [ chunk ]\ n " . join ( chunks [:4]) )

p.s: The first four chunks are First Citizen: Before we proceed any further, hear me speak.
All: Speak, speak.
First Citizen: You are all resolved rather to die than to famish?
All: Resolved.
resolved.
[chunk] First Citizen: You are all resolved rather to die than to famish?
All: Resolved.
resolved.
First Citizen: First, you know Caius Marcius is chief enemy to the people.
We know't, we know't.
[chunk] All: We know't, we know't.
First Citizen: Let us kill him, and we'll have corn at our own price.
Is't a verdict?
No more talking on't; let it be done: away, away!
[chunk] All: No more talking on't; let it be done: away, away!
Second Citizen: One word, good citizens.
Notice the overlap, and that it is (for this short part) able to split on each spoken line (separated with double newline).
Chapter 3
To work numerical with text we will need to convert the text in a vector-like representation.
Within machine learning the conversion is often called tokenization.
In the BoW representation, each word in a text is represented in one element of a (row) vector and when we have multiple texts the row vector for each text is stacked to a BoW matrix.
Then each row represents a text (a document) and each column represents a word, -an each element in the vector across the texts must correspond to the same word.

An elements of the BoW matrix can be binary indicating the presence of a word in a text, it can be represent the count of the number of times the word appear in the text, or -with further processing -it can represent a weighted count.

Consider a toy corpus with five documents with no punctuations or numbers corpus = [ " Natural language processing enables computers to understand human language " , " Transformers have revolut ionized the field of deep learning in NLP " , " Tokenization is a fundamental step in text preprocessing " , " DTU researchers are exploring multilingual models for information retrieval " , " Pretrained language models can be fine -tuned for various NLP tasks " ]
In scikit-learn, a BoW tokenization is implemented in the CountVectorizer class.
A simple scikitlearn approach could lowercasing the text and remove common words (stop words, see section 3.6.4).

computers deep dtu enables exploring field fine-tuned fundamental human information language learning models multilingual natural nlp preprocessing pretrained processing researchers retrieval revolutionized step tasks text tokenization transformers understand various
Table 3.1: Bag-of-words counts for toy corpus (zeros omitted).
for token in tok enized_t ext :
if token in vocabulary :
bow_pure [ doc_id , token_to_id [ token ]] += 1
The result is show Table 3.1.

There is a slight difference between the scikit-learn and the pure Python BoW matrix and vocabulary: "fine-tuned" is maintained as one word in the Pure Python version, while it is split in scikit-learn's tokenization into "fine" and "tune".
In the above pure Python example, a dense integer Numpy array is used to represent the BoW matrix.
In this toy problem it is not a problem, but with large datasets the scipy should be used.
The downside with the BoW representation is that words not been seen during the setup of the BoW vector cannot be represented.
They are out-of-vocabulary (OOV).
This easily happens with most texts: rare words, newly appearing proper nouns, neologisms, foreign words.

Also for languages with many forms, e.g., Estonian and/or with many suffixes, e.g., Greenlandic and Turkic may quickly have word forms in a new document that has not been seen in the corpus used to initially set up the BoW model.

div.head: Bag-of-characters or bytes

p.s: Instead of having each column represent a word as in the BoW representation, they can each instead represent a character or a byte.
Bag-of-characters or bag-of-bytes are hardly used on its own without a N-gram representation, see below.
The definition of a character is not straightforward, e.g., in old Danish (before the middle of the 19th Centery), the character å was written as aa, and in German there is ß versus ss.
Scikit-learn's CountVectorizer can be switched from the default of working on words to working with characters by the analyzer option with "char".
Here with the corpus of two documents "sos" and "us".
>>> from sklearn .
f e a t u r e _ e x t r a c t i o n .
text import C ou nt Ve c to ri ze r >>> vectorizer = C o un tV ec t or iz er ( analyzer = ' char ')

div.formula: >>> vectorizer . fit_transform ([ ' sos ' , ' us ' ]) . todense () matrix ([[1 , 2 , 0] , [0 , 1 , 1]]) >>> vectorizer . vocabulary_ { 's ': 1 , 'o ': 0 , 'u ': 2}

p.s: In an N-gram representation, you record subsequent token (typically, either words or characters) and represent the count for the combined token sequence.
For bigrams you record two subsequent tokens.
Scikit-learn's CountVectorizer is able to work with N-grams. the ngram_range controls which Ngrams should be counted.
The default ngram_range=(1, 1) means only unigram (one token).
Setting it to ngram_range=(1, 2) would yield both unigrams and bigrams.
Scikit-learn's N-gram processing interacts with the application of stop words: Stop words are excluded before the N-grams are constructed.
In the below example, the word used is added as a stop word and with ngram_range=(1, 3) unigrams, bigrams and trigrams are returned, but without the word used.
>>> from sklearn .
f e a t u r e _ e x t r a c t i o n .
text import C ou nt Ve c to ri ze r >>> vectorizer = C o un tV ec t or iz er ( ngram_range =(1 , 3) , stop_words =[ ' used ' ]) >>> vectorizer .
fit_transform ([ " She used magnetic resonance imaging " ]) .
todense () matrix ([ [1 , 1 , 1 , 1 , 1 , 1 , 1 , 1 , 1]]) >>> vectorizer .

vocabulary_ { ' she ': 6 , ' magnetic ': 1 , ' resonance ': 4 , ' imaging ': 0 , ' she magnetic ': 7 , ' magnetic resonance ': 2 , ' resonance imaging ': 5 , ' she magnetic resonance ': 8 , ' magnetic resonance imaging ': 3}
Here the bigrams "she used" and "used magnetic" are missing from the vocabulary, as well as the trigrams "she used magnetic" and "used magnetic resonance".

Scikit-learn implements HashingVectorizer where there is a fixed size of the vocabulary, the default is 1048576, and a hashing function is used to turn the token (usually a word) into the index in a vectorial representation.
Gensim implements the functionality with HashDictionary.
Its default size is only 32,000.

The advantages with hashing representation are that you can control the size of the representation quite explicitly (it is not dependent on the number of unique words in the corpus), you will not need to do an initial scan the corpus to identify the vocabulary and that new tokens (words) in unseen documents gets a representation, -the OOV problem.
The downside with the hashing representation is that you will face hash collisions.
With the default HashingVectorizer in scikit-learn you will, e.g., have hashing collisions between "wearisome" and "drummers" and between "funk", "wag" and "deserters" (Nielsen and Hansen, 2020).
In current LLMs, subword tokenization is the dominant algoritm from a text to a numerical representation.
This is a good compromise between the coverage of character representation and the "compression" of word representation.
Subword tokenization is usually learned from a corpus, although the LLM tokenization usually features a pre-tokenization stage that are rule-based with fixed regular expressions.
The method of byte-pair encoding (BPE) was originally suggested as a data compression method, going back to a publication in 1994 (Gage, 1994).
This method is together with a regular expressionbased pre-tokenization stage the dominant tokenization for LLMs.
LLM models may struggle to make simple counting tasks, e.g., counting the number of r's in the word strawberry.
The problem has been attributed to the subword tokenization (Xu and Ma, 2024).
The performance of LLMs may be better when the tokenization is based on morphologically segmentation than when it is based on BPE (Kildeberg et al., 2025).
There is the issue of whether different word (or subword) forms of the same lexeme should be "normalized" so different word forms are represented with the same token.foot_11
"Normalization" may here mean ignoring casing (lower case words), stemming, lemmatization, handling accents, etc.

This issue is particular a question when one works on the word-level with the BoW representation, whereas LLMs usually avoids this kind of normalization and let the machine learning take care of variations in word form.
Normalization is not necessarily trival.
Consider the sentence "Cafés in the US tend to serve cookies and pastries."
If we want to perform a major normalization we would lowercase "Cafés", remove its plural "s" and the accent of the "é" so we end up with "cafe" as a token.
Likewise for "cookies" and "pastries".
We can remove the "s" from cookie, but for "pastries" the "ies" should better be converted to "pastry".
Then there is the problem of "US" which could be lowercased to "us", but would then collide with a pronoun.
And perhaps the abbreviation should be expanded to "USA" or "United States of America"?
In Python, we can remove the case with the lower().foot_12
>>> text = " Caf é s in the US tend to serve cookies and pastries .
" >>> print ( text .
lower () ) caf é s in the us tend to serve cookies and pastries .
This looks simple and should work in most cases, e.g., also for Danish AEØ Å.
There is an edge case with the dotless I in Turkish that should not be converted to the dotted "i".
Furthermore other programming languages or tools than Python may be dependent on the locale for lowercasing, so that characters such as AEØ Å may not be lowercased. 3
y default the scikit-learn's CountVectorizer will lowercase words (the lowercase option).
Early small LLMs such as Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT) are available in both a uncased and a cased format.
The text-to-lexeme part of the Ordia Web application for Wikidata lexemes at https://ordia. toolforge.org/text-to-lexemes
has an option for lowercasing the first letter in each sentence maintaing the case of the rest of the letters (Nielsen, 2019).
This simple approach does not work with, e.g., "EU and USA . . .
" becoming "eU and USA . . .
In the way I use the Web application, the sentence detection often also fails, e.g., at headlines with no punctuation.
Accents and some other character formattings can be removed by the Unicode processing in Python.
>>> import unicodedata >>> def remove_a ccents ( text ) : ... normalized = unicodedata .
normalize ( ' NFKD ' , text ) ... return ' '. join ( char for char in normalized if not unicodedata .
combining ( char ) ) >>> print ( remov e_accent s ( text ) ) ' Cafes in the US tend to serve cookies and pastries .
Here the accent from Cafés is removed.
The NFKD conversion also handles ligatures (e.g., fi, where f and i are written together), superscript (e.g., ²) and some other special aspects, such that fi² becomes fi2.
Removal of accents in scikit-learn's CountVectorizer is switched on and off with the strip_accent option and is by default disabled.
If enabled, it uses the Unicode normalization with NFKD.foot_14
Gensim's deaccent also uses the Python unicodedata library but in a slightly different way than scikit-learn with NFD.foot_15
It means that Gensim does not convert, e.g., circled digit onefoot_16 to "1", but maintains it as a character.
Stemming and lemmatization attempt to remove the inflection from words.
Stemmers typically works with a heuristics while lemmatization makes a look up of words in a dictionary for the lemma form.
For English, the so-called Porter stemmer has been a popular choice.
With the implementation in NLTK.
>>> from nltk import PorterStemmer , word_tokenize >>> stemmer = PorterStemmer () >>> token_list = word_tokenize ( text ) >>> print ( " " .
join ([ stemmer . stem ( token ) for token in token_list ]) ) caf é in the us tend to serv cooki and pastri .
Here is serv reduced so much it is no longer recognizable as a verb while cooki and pastri are not normal words.
For other languages than English there is the Snowball stemmer.
For some languages, Wikidata's lexeme part covers well the different words forms.
For instance, Estonian has around 2.8 million word forms for around 83,000 lexemes (lemmas).

The NLTK lemmatizer uses WordNet to find the lemma of an English word form >>> from nltk import W o r d N e t L e m m a t i z e r >>> lemmatizer = W o r d N e t L e m m a t i z e r () >>> print ( " " .
join ([ lemmatizer . lemmatize ( token ) for token in token_list ]) ) Caf é s in the US tend to serve cooky and pastry .
Note that as the lowercasing of token was forgotten, Cafés are not lemmatized and furthermore that cooky is lemmatized somewhat special.
A hypothesis is that languages with a large number of word forms for each lexemes, benefits from stemming or lemmatization.
In the BoW model frequent words such as the, and and pronouns are sometimes removed.
These frequent words are called stop words.
What is a stop word differs among NLP tools (Nothman et al., 2018).
A list for the biomedical search engine PubMed has, e.g., the words significantly and pmid as stop words. 7
NLTK has a list of 198 English stop words and other lists for a range of other languages.
>>> from nltk .
corpus import stopwords >>> stop_words = stopwords .

words ( ' english ') >>> print ( stop_words [:10]) [ 'a ' , ' about ' , ' above ' , ' after ' , ' again ' , ' against ' , ' ain ' , ' all ' , ' am ' , ' an '] Scikit-learn has currently disabled stop word removal by default (Nothman et al., 2018).
They can be specified with the stop_word option, either with a user-specified list or with the built-in list.
>>> from sklearn .
f e a t u r e _ e x t r a c t i o n .
text import C ou nt Ve c to ri ze r >>> c o u n t _ v e c t o r i z e r = C o un tV ec t or iz er ( stop_words = ' english ') >>> c o u n t _ v e c t o r i z e r .
fit_transform ([ text ]) >>> c o u n t _ v e c t o r i z e r .
vocabulary_ { ' caf é s ': 0 , ' tend ': 4 , ' serve ': 3 , ' cookies ': 1 , ' pastries ': 2}
Here in, the, us, to, and and have been removed.

Some language such as Danish, Swedish and German can have long compounds such as vaerdipapirfinansieringstransaktionseksponering, klimatförändringskommunikation and Aufmerksamkeitsdefizithyperaktivitätsstörungen.
Decompounding splits such word into their individual compound parts.

When the Google search engine does not find a hit for vaerdipapirfinansieringstransaktionseksponering, it splits the Danish word into vaerdipapir, finansiering, and transaktionseksponering and can find some results from a modified query with these words.
Spelling errors are not rare in queries with a 2001 study finding that around 10% of words used in a query are not matched with index words (Dalianis, 2002).

Some words may appear in different forms, e.g., the Danish word for loudspeaker can be spelt with either one or two ts as højtaler or højttaler and the plural form of corona virus as koronavirus, coronavirus, koronavira, coronavira, koronavirusser and coronavirusser.
7 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/help/#help-stopwords
Chapter 4
For evaluation of natural language processing the usual suspects of machine learning are sometimes appropriate to apply.
They are precision, recall, accuracy, receiver operating characteristics, etc.

In certain applications, particular information retrieval, the evaluation of a NLP system does not necessarily need to be based on a binary match between correct an not correct, but we could instead be interested in the rank of the result.
This has led to, e.g., mean reciprocal rank.
Sometimes there is a need for evaluation of a sentence or other text entity with multiple words and where there does not need to be an exact match.
For instance, in machine translations a sentence may be translated from one language to multiple correct sentences in another languages.
Such cases has led to the introduction of "metrics" such as ROUGE.
When a text is generated one may ask a human or machine (e.g., an LLM) about how well it performance.
In NLP, scoring functions are often called metrics, but this can cause confusion among mathematicians.
In standard mathematical parlance, a metric is a function d(x, y) which satisfy NLP "metrics" often violate one or more of these axioms.
ROUGE (see section 4.6.2) is described in the English Wikipedia under the title "ROUGE (metric)", but ROUGE is not a mathematical metric (in its common form), e.g., it is not necessarily symmetric.
The cosine similarity (section 4.4) is, e.g., in the scikit-learn library group under the sklearn.metrics
submodule, but it is neither a mathematical matrics as the similarity might be negative.

div.head: Precision, recall et al.

p.s: In common machine learning, where the task is classification into one of two classes, there is a range of metrics to evaluate the performance of a system, see the comprehensive overview in the English Wikipedia at https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Precision_and_recall
which is screenshootted as Figure 4.1.
Metrics that often appear in natural language processing are precision (P), recall (R) and F1-score (F1).

div.formula: F 1 = 2 P R P + R

p.s: The more general score weighs between precision and recall.

div.formula: F β = (1 + β 2 )P R β 2 P + R

div.head: Information retrieval

p.s: In information retrieval, things get a bit more complicate as we typically return an information retrieval result in a ranked list and we want the good result as high on the top of the list or in a specific top range.
Internet search engine may use paging presenting only the top ten on the first page.
This has led to variations of the metrics above, e.g., Precision at K (precisionK), e.g., precision5 and precision10.
One common metrics for ranked results is mean reciprocal rank (MRR) This is a value between zero (worst) and one (best).

div.formula: MRR = 1 |Q| |Q| i=1 1 rank i

p.s: Here Q is a set of queries with length |Q| and rank i is the rank (indexed from one) of the relevant element in the sorted results returned.
Say, an information retrieval system should return animals based on some query and we examine two queries.
For the two search results we get sorted lists (with most relevant first) apple, cat, house, tree → rank 2 house, car, mouse, apple → rank 3, then the MRR for these two queries is

If there are multiple relevant elements in the returned list, then rank i can be taken to refer to the index of the first relevant element in the returned list apple, house, car, cat, elephant, tree → rank 4 house, car, mouse, apple → rank 3

div.head: Similarity

p.s: When two items (e.g., two documents or a query and a document) are represented in vectors, a common measure of the similarity between the two is the weighted dot products.
If we normalize the dot product with the length of each vector we get the cosine similarityfoot_17

div.formula: sim cosine (x, y) = x ′ y ||x|| 2 ||y|| 2 ,

p.s: where ||x|| 2 is the Euclidean norm, also called the L2-norm
A cosine similarity implementation appears in scikit-learn in the sklearn.metrics.pairwise
module, where it (also) work with matrix input.
With just a single matrix as input it will compute similarities across all pairs of rows.
The so-called edit distancefoot_18 measures the (minimum) distance between two strings (or other hashable sequences) in terms of insertions, deletions, substitutions and/or transpositions.
It may be used in spelling correction where a misspelt word is compared to words from a dictionary and the dictionary words with the lowest edit distances are used.
A common edit distance measure is the Levenshtein distance that measures the number of insertions, deletions and substitions.
NLTK has this implemented in the edit_distance function in the nltk.metrics.distance
submodule.
>>> import nltk >>> nltk .
edit_distance ( ' caat ' , ' cat ') # one deletion ( a ) 1 >>> nltk .
edit_distance ( ' cta ' , ' cat ') # two substitutions ( ta ) 2 >>> nltk .
edit_distance ( ' sat ' , ' cat ') # one substitutions ( ta ) 1
The NLTK function has a parameter that allows for and counts transpositions of two adjecent characters (Damerau-Levenshtein).
>>> nltk .
edit_distance ( ' cta ' , ' cat ' , transp osition s = True ) # one transposition ta -> at 1
The NLTK function also works for other types than strings, here for words >>> nltk .
edit_distance ([ ' the ' , ' beatles ' , ' sang '] , [ ' beatles ' , ' sang ' ]) # one deletion 1

Here is an example with two small ordered knowledge graphs >>> graph1 = [( ' cat ' , ' eat ' , ' mouse ') , ( ' owl ' , ' eat ' , ' mouse ') , ( ' mouse ' , ' eat ' , ' cheese ' ) ] >>> graph2 = [( ' mouse ' , ' eat ' , ' cheese ') , ( ' cat ' , ' eat ' , ' mouse ') ] >>> nltk .
edit_distance ( graph1 , graph2 ) 3 >>> nltk .
edit_distance ( graph1 , graph2 , tr ansposit ions = True ) 2 rapidfuzz is an Python library with efficient distance computation >>> from rapidfuzz .
distance import Levenshtein >>> Levenshtein .
distance ( ' caat ' , ' cat ') 1
You can set it to work on a set of words, e.g., to spell check the word "appl" against a dictionary, here by loading some hundred thousands English words via NLTK.
>>> from rapidfuzz .
distance import Levenshtein >>> from rapidfuzz import process >>> from nltk .
corpus import words >>> english_words = words .
words () # len ( english_words ) == 236736 >>> matches = process .
extract ( word , english_words , scorer = Levenshtein .

distance , limit =3) >>> print ( " Top matches : " , matches ) Top matches : [( ' apple ' , 1 , 0) , ( ' apply ' , 1 , 1) , ( ' ample ' , 2 , 2) ] The matching completes in less than 100 milliseconds.

In evaluation of language generation systems, e.g., for machine translation, we often want to compare an entire sentence (or multiple sentences) against one or more references, where we are interested that the overall meaning of the sentence and would accept some word order changes if the meaning is retained and the sentence is still grammatically correct.
The BLEU ("bilingual evaluation understudy") score goes back to 2002 (Papineni et al., 2001). 3
It is word N-gram-based and uses, in its original form, only precision.
It has a brevity penalty.
The default of BLEU uses up to 4-gram (sometimes called BLEU-4) with a uniform weight between the N-grams.
BLEU is dependent on the tokenization.
Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation (ROUGE) is a set of metrics to evaluate a string against one or more reference strings.
They is often used for evaluation of summarization or machine translation systems and suggested around 2004 (Lin, 2004).
The simplest ROUGE metric is ROUGE-1 that measures overlap in unigrams between a target and a prediction.
ROUGE-2 measures overlap with respect to bigrams.
There may be various choices when applying the ROUGE score: whether the string should lower cased, the use of a stemmer, which tokens to consider (stopwords, non-alpha-numeric).
The original reference implementation seems not to be used. 4
Google has created the rouge_score Python package (installable as rouge-scorer).
The Hugging Face evaluate.metrics.rouge
module is a wrapper around the Google implementation.foot_21
By default Google's implementation lowercase the strings and only consecutive alphanumerical characters are interpreted as tokens.foot_22
By default, stemming is applied and default stemmer is the Porter stemmer from the NLTK implementation.foot_23
By default the stemmer is not applied in the Hugging Face wrapper.
The Google implementation returns precision, recall and F1-score ("fmeasure"), while the Hugging Face wrapper only returns the F1-score.
Further confusion may come as the original paper regard the recall as the ROUGE score.
The metrics are not metrics on sets, but metrics on counts of n-grams between target and prediction strings.

Consider the following strings and the simple ROUGE-1 with the unigrams of "hello hello" (target) and "hello" (prediction) as well as "hello world hello" and (with permutated unigrams) "hello hello world".
>>> from rouge_score .
rouge_scorer import RougeScorer >>> scorer = RougeScorer ([ ' rouge1 '] , use_stemmer = False ) >>> scorer .
score ( ' hello hello ' , ' hello ') { ' rouge1 ': Score ( precision =1.0 , recall =0.5 , fmeasure = 0 .
score ( ' hello world hello ' , ' hello hello world ') { ' rouge1 ': Score ( precision =1.0 , recall =1.0 , fmeasure =1.0) }
In the first case the recall and F-measure drop as there is missing one "hello" from the prediction.
In the second case, all unigrams are there with the correct count, so even though the words have been permuted the ROUGE-1 metrics still yields 1.
As ROUGE is word-based it means that synonyms will not count.
Consider the two semantically similar sentences "He passed away yesterday evening."
and "He died last night.".
Here there is only the "He" word overlap, so the ROUGE scores will be low.
Here with Hugging Face evaluate module Here ROUGE-1 and ROUGE-L is 0.22 while ROUGE-2 is 0.

div.head: chrF++

p.s: Maja Popović's chrF is based on character N-gramsfoot_24 and its extensions add word unigrams (chrF+) or both word unigrams and word bigrams (chrF++) (Popović, 2017).
chfF++ has been used for evaluation of machine translation (Popović, 2017) and graph-to-text.foot_25
The reference implementation of chrF++foot_26 uses a character n-gram order of 6 and a word n-gram order of 2, while the β = 2.
The β parameters weigh between precision and recall.

div.formula: chrF β = (1 + β 2 ) • chrP • chrR β 2 • chrP + chrR (4.1)

p.s: With the default parameters.

div.formula: chrF = 5 • chrP • chrR 4 • chrP + chrR (4.2)

p.s: Language models can embed a word or text to a high dimensional space, where it is represented with as a vector with hundreds of elements.
LLMs such as BERT can be used for the embedding of words.
The BERTScore approach compares the embedding of the words of two texts, a reference and a candidate.

All embeddings of reference words are compared with all embeddings of the candidate words, the maximum similarity is found via the cosine similarity and weighted by term frequency-inverse document frequency (TF-IDF) to obtain the BERTScore (Zhang et al., 2020).
Compared to word-based evaluators such as ROUGE, the embedding-based scorers are less susceptible to synonym changes.
With the example from above While the ROUGE score from above was < 0.23, the results from the BERTscore, precision, recall and F1 are > 0.96.
In the example with the language set to English, The evaluate library will use a ROBERTa model ("roberta-large L17 no-idf version=0.3.12(hug
trans=4.45.2)", see the bertscore_result variable).
A multilingual BERT model is used if the language is changed to a non-English language ("bertbase-multilingual-cased L9 no-idf version=0.3.12(hug

div.head: Beyond-accuracy

p.s: The so-called beyond-accuracy scorings are values for a system, typically an information retrieval or a recommender system, that describe performances beyond the direct relevance of returned items.
Diversity, serendipity, novelty, coverage and fairness are mentioned as beyond-accuracy scores (Kaminskas and Bridge, 2016;Duricic et al., 2023).
For a list of returned items R, a diversity score can be (Kaminskas and Bridge, 2016, equation 1)

div.formula: Diversity(R) = i∈R j∈R\i dist(i, j) |R|(|R| -1) (4.3)

p.s: where dist(i, j) is some form of distance between item i and item j.
The distance could be dist(i, j) = 1 -similarity(i, j) (Smyth and McClave, 2001).
Why would we like diversity among the returned items?
If a user just search on "Mercury" then the system would not know whether the user queries for the planet, the chemical element, the Roman god or something else with this name.
It would probably be "safer" to return a diverse result set, rather than a list of items for, say, the planet.
So diversity may be of interest in ambiguous queries, but it might also be of interest for exploration (the users do not know exactly what they are searching for).
Avoiding redudancy is also important: Returning a list of copies of the same highly relevant document would degrade a user experience.

Also if your query is "Compare BMW i3 and Nissan Leaf" and the system return 10 relevant documents for further processing, but they are all about BMW i3, it would not be possible to compare the vehicles.
Serendipity and novelty are related and describe the unexpectedness of the items.
A/B testing: the evaluator is shown two different results generated by two different systems and should choose between one or the other.
The evaluator is presented with multiple results and should rank them.
Direct scoring: The evaluator give an individual output a score, e.g., just yes/no, or a scale.
The evaluator is presented with two items/results and should give an indication of the degree of relationship.
For instance, the evaluator is presented with two words and should give a value for their similarity.
The evaluator is presented with a set of results and should determine the odd one out
The evaluator is presented with three items and should determine a fourth by analogies between the words.
To evaluate systems for lexical semantics, datasets exist where humans have scored word pairs for relatedness or similarity.
The semantic relation between two sense can be characterized by similarity and relatedness.
In semantic lexicography there is usually a distinction between the two: relatedness is a relation with general association between the two senses, while similarity is a class-like relatedness.
Consider the concept of coffee (drink).
This may be highly related to americano, tea, coffee cup and coffee machine.
Whereas coffee may only be highly similar to americano and tea and not similar to a coffee cup nor a coffee machine.
This is because coffee can be regarded as a type or a subclass of drinkable liquid and so can americano and tea, where as a coffee cup or a coffee machine is not of the type.
In the case with similarity and relatedness, the datasets are often where there is a set of pairs and each pair is scored for relatedness/similarity.
If a human has scored the pairs then it is often regarded as ground truth.
When compared to other scoring of a system, the overall performances are usually computed with a correlation, either pearson, Kendall og Spearmann.
If we ask an evaluator (human or machine) to evaluate the performance of a NLP system that generates a text what features/dimensions/aspects/metrics should we ask about?

It could be an overall quality, but numerous more granular features have presented under different names (Zhong et al., 2022;Liu et al., 2023;Frisoni et al., 2022):foot_27 Coherence, commonsense, compliance, consistency, correctness, coverage, engagingness, factuality, fluency, grammar, groundedness, hallucinations, likeability, naturalness, relevance, redundancy, repetitiveness, style, text structure, typicality and perhaps others.
For an evaluation of coherence a part of an example prompt for a summarization task may read (Liu et al., 2023) Coherence (1-5) -the collective quality of all sentences.
We align this dimension with the DUC quality question of structure and coherence whereby "the summary should be wellstructured and well-organized.
The summary should not just be a heap of related information, but should build from sentence to sentence to a coherent body of information about a topic."
From the same authors engagingness would be prompted with (Liu et al., 2023) Engagingness (1-3) Is the response dull/interesting?
A score of 1 (dull) means that the response is generic and dull.
